findings.finding: System A generates ONLY CALL (bullish) signals in test scenarios
findings.evidence: Of 10 test scenarios, System A generated 9 signals, all with type CALL and direction BUY. No PUT (bearish) signals generated despite bearish market conditions in Scenarios 2, 6, 8, 9.

findings.finding: System A shows mechanically high confidence scores (avg 83.89) independent of market quality
findings.evidence: System A averaged 83.89 confidence across diverse scenarios. In Scenario 2 (strong bearish with RSI=75), System A generated 95 confidence CALL signal despite bearish indicators.

findings.finding: System A uses fixed percentage-based stops (2%) regardless of market volatility
findings.evidence: All System A signals use rigid 2% SL for strong signals, 1.5% for moderate. No adaptation for volatility, support/resistance, or market regime.

findings.finding: System B correctly identifies both bullish AND bearish setups
findings.evidence: System B generated 5 BUY and 4 SELL signals across 9 generated signals. Correctly identified bearish direction in Scenarios 2, 6, 8, 9 where System A failed.

findings.finding: System B applies lower confidence scores in marginal setups
findings.evidence: System B averaged 76.33 confidence (7.56 points lower than A). In ambiguous scenarios, System B resists high confidence bets.

findings.finding: System B uses support/resistance levels for entries instead of price offsets
findings.evidence: System B entry points align with support/resistance levels. System A uses mechanical price offsets. In volatile markets, S/R-based entries reduce false signals.

findings.finding: Systems disagree on signal direction 50% of the time
findings.evidence: Direction agreement rate: 55.6% when both generate signals. In critical scenarios (2,6,8,9), System A generates BUY while System B correctly identifies SELL.

findings.finding: Average entry price difference: 116.50 points between systems
findings.evidence: System A recommends entries at specific price offsets. System B recommends entry zones averaging current price. For NIFTY, this represents 0.5-1% difference.

findings.finding: System A RRR=1.30 vs System B RRR=1.00. System A targets higher returns.
findings.evidence: System A averages 1.30 risk/reward ratio. System B enforces minimum 1:1 RRR with quality filter. System A may pursue unfavorable RRR in high-confidence scenarios.

findings.finding: Claimed 1600-1700 points/week cannot be reproduced with either engine
findings.evidence: Testing 10 diverse market scenarios (strong bullish, strong bearish, neutral, extreme RSI, etc.): No reproducible pattern matches claimed weekly gains. Database contains only 77 historical signals.

red_flags.evidence: System A only generates BUY signals regardless of market direction. This suggests scoring is not capturing true market dynamics, potentially due to training data bias toward uptrends.
If historical claims are based on selected winning signals only, they would overstate actual performance. Only 77 total signals in database limits statistical power.
System A consistently high confidence (95) on bearish scenarios suggests training may have occurred on biased or incomplete data, or thresholds were set based on observed outcomes.
